speaker: interviewer
text: It is October 29, 1996. Our liberator is Warren Dunn. My name is Renee Firestone. We are in Monterey, California, in the United States, and we will speak English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It is October 29, 1996. My name is Renee Firestone. R-E-N-double E-F-I-R-E-S-T-O-N-E. It is my pleasure this morning to interview a liberator of Dachau, Mr. Warren Dunn in Monterey, California, in the United States, and we will speak English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Good morning, Colonel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Renee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us your full name and spell it for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Warren Courtland Dunn. W-A-R-R-E-N, middle name, C-O-U-R-T-L-A-N-D, last name, D-U-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us where were you born and when.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Fort Lewis, Washington, July 14, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us your father's name, and tell us what his occupation was and what you remember of him most?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Clarence, middle name, Albert, Dunn. He, most of his career, was in the military. And he went in when he was 17 and shortly after the-- his induction into the military, he was assigned as a-- an instructor of ROTC, Reserve Officers Training Corps, in Pullman, Washington at the Washington State College, now Washington State University, and spent from, I think it was 1926 to 1930 there in Pullman. And then he was reassigned to Santa Barbara, California in the-- in the same job as an ROTC instructor. In fact, he was Director of ROTC at Santa Barbara High School. And that's where I was really brought up, was in-- in Santa Barbara. So he spent most of his military career, or a lot of it, in Santa Barbara as an ROTC instructor. When World War II started, he had been a-- a reserve officer. He got his commission then in 1942 and was reassigned to Southern California, where he was the so-called Professor of Military Science and Tactics at Hollywood High School and Fremont High Schools and Van Nuys High School, where I resumed my education. That was traumatic. [LAUGHS] Having to move from all my friends in Santa Barbara, and in my-- in my senior year, and go to school at Van Nuys High School and make new friends, but I-- I did. And I really enjoyed my senior year in-- in Van Nuys, as reluctant as I was to move. But then he-- he continued on as-- in this job at these three high schools. And then he was assigned to-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I can't think of the name of the fort he was assigned to on the East Coast. Fort Ethan Allen, Vermont. That's where he was assigned. And he-- he and my mother spent three years at that camp. Then he-- he went to Japan on General MacArthur's staff in 1951, well after the war. And came back in '54 and retired from the military. And a year later my mother passed away. And so he-- he spent his retirement years in-- in Woodland Hills in Southern California and in Canoga Park. He remarried after my mother passed away, about two years later, and spent almost-- well, the rest of his retirement years until he passed away in-- in '75, '76. And that's essentially what my dad did. He was a-- a career officer, a career military man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a father was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Strict. Let me tell you about a-- it was an experience that I'll never forget. I was in the ROTC at Van Nuys High School, and of course, he was the-- he was the officer in charge there. And we were vying to become officers in the ROTC. And so we had to go through these drills, and I was assigned this day to be a leader of one of the platoons, and he was the so-called company commander.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he gave these commands, and I gave the-- he said, company mass left. And I said, continue to march, and he said, as you were. Finally, after the third time, he said, Dunn, what is the proper command when I give the command company mass left? And I said, sir, is it not continue to march and then half left march? Oh, yes. And he never, ever apologized to me, because he was very strict, but most of the time, quite fair. And I loved him a great deal. Did you have any interaction with him? Did you do things together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One in particular. I had-- when I was, I guess, 11, one I'll always remember, because it was so-- such a nice experience. I had had my appendix out, and he came to the hospital and he said, when you-- when you're well again, we're going to go to the Rose Bowl. And so we went together with a friend of his to-- to the Rose Bowl. I think USC must have been playing somebody, Tennessee I think it was. I believe so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- I had the most glorious experience going to the Rose Bowl and seeing the-- seeing the Rose Parade and the Rose Bowl. And after that, we went and had fried chicken somewhere over in West LA. There was a-- a-- a restaurant, Dinah's Shack. Do you remember Dinah's Shack?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a Dinah's Shack over somewhere near Westwood that we went to because they had-- they were reported to have the best fried chicken there ever was, and we enjoyed that. So I-- that was a-- a most memorable experience that I'll-- I'll never forget with my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, very gentle person. We were together a lot. As a matter of fact, I thoroughly enjoyed cooking with her. And I-- I helped her wash the clothes. And-- and we-- we were very close, my mother and sister and she were very close. And almost every year we would go to Portland, Oregon together, my sister, and my mother and I, because my grandmother lived there. She was very close to her mother. And so we always, during the summer, went to Portland. And I was-- thoroughly enjoyed Portland, Oregon. All the-- the freshness of it, the woods, and the swimming pools, and Jantzen Beach, and-- and the-- the Ferris wheel and the roller coaster there at Jantzen Beach. And we spent a couple of months every summer, almost every summer, going up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leone. Leone [? Waite, ?] and her maiden name was Crepps, of German descent. And my mother eventually, unfortunately, she passed away in her 50s, in 1955, and that was pretty traumatic. She died of cancer. We were awfully close. She-- she spent most of her life as-- I think when I talked to you earlier, that-- that she was a-- I think I said, just a housewife, but that's-- that's a poor use of the word just. And she was a housewife and loved to cook, loved to sew, and a very, very tender and loving person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father strict with her too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- they-- most of the time, they-- they weren't strict. They-- they had a lot of friends, particularly in Santa Barbara. He being part of the-- of the-- the faculty, since he was the ROTC instructor, they had a lot of friends, played a lot of bridge together with their friends and a lot of entertaining back and forth. And so they, I think they enjoyed each other quite a bit. She was, as I said, very attached to her mother, so there was that. I don't know whether that was a-- came between them or not, but in-- in-- in the main, they-- they were well suited for one another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have a-- a sister. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about your sister. What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Betty Cosgrove. She's-- she's married to a-- he's a painting contractor. And at the same-- near the same time that I was married to my first wife, she married Richard, Dick, Cosgrove, Thomas Richard Cosgrove. And she-- she went to Van Nuys High School. She's three years younger than I am. Went to Van Nuys High School. She was there at the same time I was, just before I graduated and went on to UCLA. She really, again, was a housewife. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Did some-- some jobs. She was a sales person at a-- in a department store, I think, in Van Nuys. But in the-- in the main, she-- she was-- took care of the home, and her husband painted all over Southern California. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you two close when you were children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, quite close. We have drifted apart in our later years. Of course, she lives there, and-- she lives now in-- in Tehachapi. They bought a house in Tehachapi, so she lives there. She lived in, and her husband lived in, Woodland Hills, not too far from my parents for a number of years, [BACKGROUND NOISE] until my father moved to Canoga Park. And like I said, she's-- she's now 69 and waiting for the big 7-0. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your school years in Santa Barbara?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much. Yes. I-- I really enjoyed school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we moved to-- moved to Santa Barbara, moved there in 1929, and I started grammar school in the first grade at a-- at a school called Roosevelt, Roosevelt Grammar School, Elementary School. Went through grammar school there and then went to La Cumbre Junior High School. We had moved from the-- the original house we lived in on Panchita Place in Santa Barbara-- no, I'm sorry, Sola Street, in Santa Barbara, to Panchita Place, and then to Anapamu, which was right across the street from the high school. Well, Sola Street was not too far from-- from La Cumbre Junior High School. Anapamu was right across the street. So-- so we moved to Anapamu, and I went to junior high school all the way across town. When-- when I went to high school, we moved over right near the junior high school, so I went all the way across town to high school. But I thoroughly enjoyed school. I was a good student, A student in-- in high school, and thoroughly enjoyed it. I was-- enjoyed math and-- and science and art. I liked art. And I-- I-- I believe my high school experience was really good. I was in ROTC at Santa Barbara also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a popular young man?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a bit shy, so I-- I don't know that I was-- I had a lot of friends though, a lot of friends. I palled around with-- with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] guys. I had a-- a particular friend that lived a couple of blocks from me, and-- and we played two-man football, and we challenged every other two-man football team that we could find, and we always won.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Bill McGuinness. Billy McGuinness. And we-- we just palled around all the time. And then in school, I had these other friends, that we were together at school. One of them was named Vince [? Garno, ?] and he-- he kept my '29 Ford, when I was in high school, my-- my 1929 two-door sedan in-- in good shape. In fact, he hopped it up [LAUGHS] so I could race with other guys. And another was Dick [? Dockposo, ?] Jimmy Pike, Bill [? Herdman. ?] Those were some of my friends. I really had a-- I think, a wonderful childhood. I remember-- I don't remember really anything negative at all about my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep up the friendship with them later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a while. After we moved from Santa Barbara to Van Nuys, I-- I tried to stay in contact with them. I went back to Santa Barbara on occasion and looked them up. But after a while, that-- that faded, and I had made new friends at Van Nuys, and a lot of new friends when I went to UCLA. Lived in Sherman Oaks, and I-- we had a carpool that went to UCLA from that-- from the valley, from San Fernando Valley. So I made a lot of new friends there and a lot of new friends in-- in ROTC at UCLA.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult for you to make these changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah. That-- that was very traumatic, moving from Santa Barbara. Very. Because yeah, I'd-- I'd lived there most of my life, from when I was four until I was 16. And all of a sudden to be uprooted and go to a strange place was pretty traumatic for me. I had a hard time with it. And I even wanted to stay in Santa Barbara and maybe stay with one of my friends, and-- but my mother and dad didn't want that to happen. They wanted me with them. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you date? Did you have a girlfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had one that I was-- sort of dated. In fact, we went to the ROTC Ball one year, and I was-- I was very enthralled with her. I'm not sure that she was totally enthralled with me, but she did go with me to the ROTC Ball, and we had a good time. Then I had-- I dated some in Van Nuys. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One-- one lady's name was Betty Calgary. As a matter of fact, we went to our 50th reunion, and I met her again. Of course, with Marge this time, and boy, had she changed. [LAUGHS] But she was a doll in high school, a real doll. I-- I-- I liked her a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have so many talents. What were your hobbies when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Model building. I-- I made things out of wood. Really, I've-- I've loved wood most of my life. And art. I like to draw. As a matter of fact, Marge has-- has some of my drawings that I made when I was a youngster. Sports. I love baseball, love football, swimming. I learned to swim when I was a Boy Scout, when I was 11. That was a-- a hard-- a hard learn though. [LAUGHS] We were on a-- a-- a camp, an encampment one year, northeast of Santa Barbara, in the Santa Ynez Valley. And we were camped near the Santa Ynez River, and I didn't really know how to swim very well. I'd taken some lessons, but. One of the guys that-- that was sort of my overseer-- he was older than I was-- Jerry was his name. He said, jump in, and I'll head for that rock over there. And so it was probably no more than 20 feet wide there. I got over to this rock, and somebody had come in in front of me, so I couldn't get out. And so I started floundering around in the water, and Jerry jumped in and hauled me out, and then he threw me back in the water. [LAUGHS] And I learned to swim. [LAUGHS] And I became a pretty good swimmer from then on.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the school, do you remember any teachers that made a great impression on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in-- in grammar school particularly, there was this teacher who I dearly loved. And she-- we hit it off quite well. As a matter of fact, she had a-- and her husband had a farm [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] out in somewhere near Santa Ynez, in Santa Ynez Valley. And she would often invite us, my family and sister and I, to come. We'd go and have dinner there, and she was a-- she was-- I was very impressed with her. A lovely, lovely person. I-- I-- Her name-- her name slips. I can't-- can't quite remember her name. But I-- she-- she really impressed me. Another was my math teacher in Santa Barbara High School. He was an excellent teacher. And I-- I really think I owe a lot of-- of my study ability to him. And he was a good friend of my dad too, and that didn't hurt. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was quite impressed with him. I don't remember his name either, but I certainly was impressed with him. And in-- in college, university, there was a-- a science teacher who was different than any teacher I had ever known. He-- he used the vernacular a lot, and he was a bit profane, but I enjoyed him. He was just really a good teacher. And he would often say, why the hell did I do that? Oh. Oh, I see [LAUGHS] I-- I-- I was impressed with him. What university did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: UCLA, University of California at Los Angeles. And I thoroughly enjoyed UCLA too, although I had a hard time. Since I was a-- a-- a cock-sure high school A student, you know, I go to UCLA, and my first introduction in a chemistry class is to 500 students sitting in a-- this huge theater, and you could hardly see the instructor. He's way down there, and there's 500 of us, and he doesn't give a-- a hoot whether we learn or not. And I had a hard time in my first semester at UCLA. And then I went in the service. So I went in and started UCLA in-- in '42 and went into the service in early '43, so I didn't really finish my first year, although I got credit for it. And I didn't resume my college education until after the war. I came back in '46 and went back to UCLA.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still had a hard time because I had forgotten how to study. And I had a-- a counselor that I went to. And I told him that I was having a hard time studying. And he-- he asked me, well, how do-- how do you do it? How do you study?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, well, I-- I try to-- I read the textbooks, and then I try to remember what I read. And he said, well, you know, what you really need to do is-- is read through and make notes, and then read it again, and then read it again for total context. And ever after that, I-- I was OK with my studies. As a matter of fact, I did quite well at UCLA, where I graduated in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me what religion are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I was Presbyterian. Previous to that, I was sort of Christian Science, because my mother, she-- she became kind of chronically ill in her late '40s, and so we went to the Christian Science Church, which I didn't like very much. And then I went to-- after that, she-- she left it also-- and I went and became a Presbyterian, and I'm now a Methodist. As a matter of fact, I-- Marge and I attend the First United Methodist in Pacific Grove, right down the street a ways, and we've-- we been at that-- in that church for three years now. We-- we-- we were married in the Episcopal church. And we went to that church for awhile, but the liturgy was a little bit much for-- for us, and so we looked for another church and have-- have now wound up at a Methodist Church.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, was you family a church-going family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. My-- my mother occasionally was, and she got into Christian Science, and primarily because she wanted to-- to not let her-- her illness get in the way of her-- she wanted to-- to make sure there wasn't a-- a mind thing, so she chose Christian Science. And-- but they were not really what you'd term church goers. Just occasionally. My dad really-- didn't really go to church much. He became very, very much so though later in the Presbyterian Church after my mother passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: He was still in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that-- this was after he retired. And after my mother had passed away, and he-- he remarried this Ruth, that you saw the picture of her. He got very involved in the Presbyterian Church in Sherman Oaks and did a lot of good work with the church. He was an elder, as a matter of fact, until he passed away in '76-- '77.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you a church-going man now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, ma'am. Yeah. I-- I do a lot of things for the church. As a matter of fact, I just recently redid the doors to the sanctuary. They're huge, 10-foot doors, and-- and-- they were getting pretty-- pretty dilapidated, cracks in them. And so I'm a-- a member of the board of trustees of that church, and we got to talking about how we should-- should fix those doors, because they're-- they're very valuable and very beautiful. They have tile insets, little pieces of tile that tell a story, on these doors. They're gorgeous. And so I volunteered to figure out how to-- what to do. So I went to one of our local paint stores, and they turned one of their consultants over to me, and he came and looked at the door, and he said, well, this is what you need to do. Clean the doors, prime them, use some elastomeric paint on them that-- that will fill the cracks, and they won't-- they'll give with the weather, the heat and the-- the cold, and they won't re-crack.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop to change the tape now. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll--
